narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 37.50, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24, and decile rankings average around 6 in both early and later years, showing how your profile stacks up against those who succeeded. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile ranking, followed by your Year 1 decile, with your LSAT score playing a counterbalancing role. Your Year 3 decile of 2 had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, as it signals performance well below what typically leads to passing among admitted students. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2 reinforced that concern, indicating early academic challenges that the model sees as persistent risks. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 37.50, which is slightly above the average for bar passers at 37.27, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success. Your middle family income and full-time status aligned reasonably with successful students, where full-time is the norm at 93% and middle income represents about 35%, so they didn't sway the outcome much. Overall, the model weighed your lower deciles as bigger red flags than your solid LSAT compensated for, compared to typical accepted students whose stronger class rankings tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's just how these patterns from past bar takers shaped the call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these exact improvements.
